title: Add Constant to Linear Term
description: Combine a linear term and a constant to compute total cost or value.
condition: An expression of the form a·n + b with given values for a, n, and b.
response: Compute a·n and then add b.
label: Principle / Model

title: Combine Like Terms
description: Add or subtract coefficients of terms that have the same variable part to simplify an expression.
condition: An expression contains two or more terms with identical variable parts (e.g., ax and bx, or ay and by).
response: Add or subtract the coefficients and keep the common variable part.
label: Principle / Model
example: Combine $7x - 2x$ to get $(7 - 2)x = 5x$, and $10y + 8y = 18y$, so $7x + 10y - 2x + 8y = 5x + 18y$.
notes: Relies on the distributive property and properties of operations.

title: Define Variable Meaning in Context
description: State what an algebraic variable represents in a real-world situation.
condition: A contextual problem modeled with a variable (e.g., x in a cost equation).
response: Explicitly state what quantity the variable represents in the situation.
label: Rule / Plan
example: For $36x + 17 = 468$, state: "$x$ represents the cost of each workbook."

title: Distribute a Negative Across Parentheses
description: Apply subtraction or multiplication by −1 to each term inside parentheses.
condition: An expression of the form A − (B + C) or −1(B + C).
response: Change the sign of each term inside the parentheses.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Special case of the distributive property with −1.

title: Divide Both Sides of Equation
description: Isolate a variable by dividing both sides of an equation by the same nonzero number.
condition: An equation of the form a·x = c where a ≠ 0.
response: Divide both sides by a to get x = c/a.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $4x=12$, divide by 4 to get $x=3$.

title: Factor Out the Greatest Common Factor
description: Rewrite a sum of terms as a product by factoring out their greatest common factor (GCF).
condition: An expression where all terms share a common numerical and/or variable factor.
response: Divide each term by the GCF and write the expression as GCF(parenthetical expression).
label: Principle / Model
notes: Students may factor out a negative instead (e.g., -6(2a - 5b + 3)), which is equivalent.

title: Interpret Equals Sign as Total Amount
description: Understand that the equals sign indicates the total value represented by an expression.
condition: An equation of the form expression = r representing a situation or diagram.
response: Interpret r as the total amount represented by the expression.
label: Concept
example: In $5(x + 4) = 80$, the 80 represents the total of the five equal parts.

title: Interpret Inequality Solution in Context
description: Translate the algebraic solution of an inequality into a meaningful statement about the real-world situation.
condition: Given a solved inequality representing a contextual situation (e.g., x > 10 where x is weeks).
response: State what the solution means in context, including units and reference to the situation.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If $x > 10$ and $x$ is weeks, then the farmer will have less than 50 cubic yards of sawdust after 10 weeks.
notes: Requires connecting algebraic result back to quantities and units in the problem.

title: Model Total with Additional Constant
description: Translate a situation with a per-unit cost and an additional fixed fee into an equation of the form px + q = r.
condition: A context describing p equal groups at cost x each plus an additional fixed amount q, with total r given.
response: Write an equation of the form px + q = r.
label: Principle / Model
example: 36 workbooks at $x dollars each plus $17 shipping totals $468: \(36x + 17 = 468\).

title: Multiply by Reciprocal
description: Solve equations with fractional coefficients by multiplying both sides by the reciprocal.
condition: An equation of the form (1/a)x = c or a fraction multiplied by an expression equals a number.
response: Multiply both sides by a (the reciprocal) to isolate x.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $\frac{1}{3}x=-12$, multiply both sides by 3 to get $x=-36$.

title: Recognize Equivalent Linear Forms
description: Distinguish between expressions of the form ax + b and a(x + b) and interpret structural differences.
condition: Two algebraic expressions with and without parentheses (e.g., 3x+6 vs. 3(x+6)).
response: Identify whether the constant is inside or outside the multiplication and interpret accordingly.
label: Principle / Model
example: $3x+6$ means three groups plus 6; $3(x+6)$ means three groups of (x+6).

title: Represent Repeated Groups with Multiplication
description: Recognize that multiple equal groups of the same expression are represented using multiplication.
condition: A diagram or situation shows p equal groups, each containing the same quantity (e.g., x + k).
response: Write or interpret the total as p(x + k).
label: Principle / Model
example: Five equal parts each labeled $x + 4$ are represented as $5(x + 4)$.

title: Reverse Inequality When Dividing by a Negative
description: When solving an inequality, reverse the inequality symbol if multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number.
condition: An inequality is multiplied or divided by a negative value during solving.
response: Reverse the direction of the inequality symbol.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $-7x < -70$, divide by $-7$ and reverse the sign to get $x > 10$.
notes: Follows from order properties of real numbers.

title: Solve Equation with Variable in Parentheses
description: Solve equations of the form p(x + q) = r by dividing or distributing, then isolating the variable.
condition: Equation of the form p(x + q) = r.
response: Either divide both sides by p first or distribute p, then isolate x.
label: Principle / Model
example: Solve $2(a + 3.6) = 44$: divide by 2 to get $a + 3.6 = 22$, then subtract 3.6.

title: Solve Two-Step Linear Equation
description: Solve an equation requiring two inverse operations to isolate the variable.
condition: An equation of the form ax + b = c or a(x + b) = c.
response: Use inverse operations (subtract/add, then divide/multiply) to solve for x.
label: Principle / Model
example: Solve $4x+8=20$: subtract 8 → $4x=12$, divide by 4 → $x=3$.

title: Solve Two-Step Linear Inequality
description: Isolate the variable in a linear inequality of the form px + q < r or px + q > r using inverse operations.
condition: Given a linear inequality in one variable with two operations (e.g., px + q < r).
response: Use inverse operations to isolate the variable and express the solution as an inequality.
label: Principle / Model
example: Solve $-7x + 120 < 50$: subtract 120 to get $-7x < -70$, then divide by $-7$ and reverse the sign to obtain $x > 10$.
notes: Includes applying the negative-division principle when necessary.

title: Subtract Constant from Both Sides
description: Use inverse addition to isolate a variable term in a linear equation.
condition: An equation of the form x + b = c or ax + b = c.
response: Subtract b from both sides.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $4x+8=20$, subtract 8 to get $4x=12$.

title: Translate Context to Linear Equation
description: Represent a verbal situation with a linear equation in one variable.
condition: A word problem describing a total as a combination of equal groups and/or constants.
response: Write an equation of the form ax + b = c or a(x + b) = c that matches the context.
label: Principle / Model
example: Four equal weights plus 8 grams equals 20 grams → $4x + 8 = 20$.

title: Translate Equal-Groups Situation to Equation
description: Represent a word problem describing equal daily amounts and a total as a multiplication equation.
condition: Word problem describing a constant daily amount over a fixed number of days totaling a known sum.
response: Write an equation of the form n(base + x) = total.
label: Rule / Plan
example:
Andre runs 7 days at $(3.1 + x)$ miles each for 35 miles total: 
$7(3.1 + x) = 35$.
notes: Modeling convention linking repeated addition to multiplication.

title: Write Linear Inequality from Context
description: Translate a real-world situation with a decreasing or increasing quantity and a comparison statement into a linear inequality.
condition: Given a context describing an initial amount, a constant rate of change, and a comparison such as 'less than' or 'greater than.'
response: Define a variable and write a linear inequality representing the situation.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Let $x$ be the number of weeks. The amount of sawdust left is $120 - 7x$. For less than 50 cubic yards: $120 - 7x < 50$.
notes: Requires identifying initial value, rate, and inequality phrase.
